retrieved_docs.title: Massive Transfusion -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Criteria for stopping a major transfusion protocol differ by site. According to the American College of Surgeons' practice guidelines, clinicians can stop a massive transfusion protocol or switch to goal-directed transfusion for patients based on the following lab parameters if they are no longer actively bleeding: Stop RBC transfusions for a hemoglobin of 10 g/dL or higher. Stop plasma transfusions for a PT less than 18 seconds and aPTT less than 35 seconds. Stop platelet transfusions for platelet counts higher than 150 x10 9 /µL. Stop cryoprecipitate or fibrinogen concentrate for fibrinogen levels higher than 180 g/L.

retrieved_docs.title: Massive Transfusion -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the criteria and indications for activating a massive transfusion protocol in emergencies. Implement massive transfusion protocols promptly and effectively during emergencies by adhering to established guidelines to ensure the rapid and appropriate delivery of blood products. Apply point-of-care coagulation testing, such as thromboelastography, to guide and adjust blood product transfusions, ensuring patient safety and minimizing the risk of transfusion-related complications. Communicate clearly with the interprofessional healthcare team and blood bank personnel to maintain seamless coordination throughout all phases of a massive transfusion. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Anemia Screening -- Results, Reporting, and Critical Findings -- Red Blood Cell Transfusion Guidelines
retrieved_docs.content: "Transfusion of RBCs should be based on the patient's clinical condition. Indications for RBC transfusion include acute sickle cell crisis or acute blood loss of greater than 1,500 cc or 30% of blood volume. Patients with symptomatic anemia should be transfused if they cannot function without treating the anemia."

retrieved_docs.title: Determining the Need for Blood Transfusion -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: To decrease known morbidity and mortality risks, transfusion guidelines based on evidence-based data have been developed. Results demonstrate that these guidelines can lead to fewer transfusions, better patient outcomes, and, concomitantly, cost reductions for hospitals. Guidelines alone cannot ensure these benefits, as they must be accompanied by physician education and clinical consideration. Additionally, many transfusion strategies recommend the transfusion of one unit and checking lab values to assess effectiveness. This minimizes the risk of over-transfusion as well as circulatory overload.

retrieved_docs.title: Anemia Screening -- Results, Reporting, and Critical Findings -- Red Blood Cell Transfusion Guidelines
retrieved_docs.content: The authors cite an updated Cochrane review supporting a restrictive transfusion trigger in patients with noncardiac disorders. The review advocates a hemoglobin threshold of 7 g/dL rather than the previously used 10 g/dL. Statistical analysis supports this revised threshold, showing a 54% relative decrease in the number of transfused blood units and an associated reduction in 30-day mortality rates with the updated criterion. [29] [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Blood Transfusion -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Unless the patient is actively bleeding, it is recommended to transfuse 1 unit of packed red cells at a time, which will typically increase the hemoglobin value by 1 g/dL and hematocrit by 3%. Follow up by checking post-transfusion hemoglobin. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Exchange Transfusion -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Exchange transfusion necessitates a multidisciplinary approach. Healthcare professionals must possess advanced skills in identifying suitable indications for exchange transfusion, choosing the appropriate technique, and effectively managing potential complications. Effective communication and collaborative strategies are paramount to ensure thorough patient preparation and vigilant monitoring during the procedure. Developing effective strategies, including establishing protocols, guidelines, and risk mitigation protocols, is crucial. Interprofessional communication is vital to keep all team members updated, foster collaboration, and address emerging concerns. Care coordination among healthcare professionals ensures smooth transitions and consistent care, enhancing patient-centered care, reducing morbidity, improving patient safety, and optimizing team performance throughout the exchange transfusion process.

retrieved_docs.title: Blood Transfusion -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Guidelines on red blood cell transfusion from the American Association of Blood Banks advise a restrictive approach for stable patients with non-hemorrhaging anemia. [6] While there could be variations, anemia is usually defined as a hemoglobin level of less than 13 g/dL in males and less than 12 g/dL in females. While currently, a more restrictive threshold is used to determine the indication for transfusion, previously, a liberal strategy, typically using a cutoff of hemoglobin less than 10 g/dL, was used, regardless of symptoms.

retrieved_docs.title: Platelet Transfusion -- Indications -- Specific Prophylactic Transfusion Thresholds
retrieved_docs.content: Platelet transfusion is not routinely indicated before bone marrow biopsy, peripheral or central catheter insertion, traction removal of tunneled central venous catheters, and cataract removal. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Blood Transfusion -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the indications for blood transfusions. Describe the management of blood transfusion complications. Explain the importance of proper pre-transfusion preparation of donor blood to reduce the risk of complications. Review a structured interprofessional team approach to provide effective care to and appropriate surveillance of patients undergoing blood transfusion. retrieved_docs.title: Blood Transfusion -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: The American Society of Anesthesiologists advises transfusion at hemoglobin levels of 6 g/dL or less. However, more recent data show decreased mortality with preanesthetic hemoglobin greater than 8 g/dL, especially in renal transplant patients. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Determining the Need for Blood Transfusion -- Function -- Red Blood Cells
retrieved_docs.content: The literature strongly supports adhering to a restrictive transfusion strategy (7 g/dL) in hospitalized adult and pediatric intensive care patients who are hemodynamically stable. [1] [2] The evidence is not as compelling for patients with cardiovascular disease, but recommendations are to adhere to a restrictive strategy (hemoglobin 8 gm/dL) for patients with preexisting cardiovascular disease. There is insufficient evidence to make recommendations for patients with acute coronary syndrome.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1768
retrieved_docs.content: Acute posthemorrhagic anemia is a specific form of فقر الدم (anemia) that arises rapidly after substantial blood loss, leading to a sudden decrease in circulating red blood cells and hemoglobin levels. As a subtype of anemia, it shares the fundamental characteristic of reduced oxygen‑carrying capacity of the blood, but its acute onset distinguishes it from chronic or nutritional anemias. Prompt recognition and treatment of acute posthemorrhagic anemia are essential in patient care, as rapid restoration of intravascular volume and red cell mass—through fluid resuscitation, blood transfusion, and control of the bleeding source—are required to prevent tissue hypoxia, organ dysfunction, and hemodynamic instability. Understanding that acute posthemorrhagic anemia falls under the broader category of فقر الدم helps clinicians classify the condition, anticipate its pathophysiology, and apply appropriate therapeutic strategies within the overall framework of anemia management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 421
retrieved_docs.content: Blood transfusion, known in Arabic as نقل الدم, is a medical procedure used to replace lost blood components and is classified under the broader concept of blood replacement therapy. It encompasses specific subtypes, including نقل الدم الذاتي (autologous blood transfusion), which involves collecting and reinfusing the patient’s own blood. Although blood transfusion is a valuable therapeutic intervention, it can also be a cause of complications related to surgical procedures or other medical care, underscoring the importance of careful indication, thorough screening, and vigilant monitoring to minimize adverse outcomes. Recognizing the hierarchical relationship between general blood transfusion, its child category autologous transfusion, and the potential for procedural complications is essential for safe and effective patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 2 -- Community 698
retrieved_docs.content: Blood substitutes and plasma protein fractions comprise several distinct agents, including O‑raffinose cross‑linked human hemoglobin, dextran, and hydroxyethyl starch. These products are employed as volume expanders or oxygen‑carrying solutions in clinical settings where conventional blood transfusion is unavailable or unsuitable. While each agent belongs to the same therapeutic category, hydroxyethyl starch carries a specific safety limitation: it is contraindicated in patients with thrombotic disease (الاعتلال الخثري). Recognizing this contraindication is essential for clinicians to avoid exacerbating clot‑related conditions when selecting a blood substitute or plasma protein fraction for patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2737
retrieved_docs.content: Dextran 75, a high‑molecular‑weight plasma volume expander, is contraindicated in patients with الاعتلال الخثري (thrombotic disease); using dextran 75 in this condition may promote clot formation and worsen clinical outcomes, so clinicians should avoid prescribing it to individuals who have thrombotic disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 4 -- Community 2809
retrieved_docs.content: Elective termination of pregnancy (إنهاء الحمل - اختياري) is clinically associated with several obstetric conditions, including gestational diabetes (داء السكري أثناء الحمل), threatened miscarriage (تهديد بالإجهاض), and iron‑deficiency anemia (فقر الدم الناتج عن نقص الحديد). These associations highlight the importance of evaluating and managing metabolic, hematologic, and early‑pregnancy complications when counseling patients about elective pregnancy termination, ensuring comprehensive risk assessment and optimal maternal health outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2246
retrieved_docs.content: Tromethamin is categorized within the therapeutic class of Blood Products/Modifiers/Volume Expanders, indicating that it functions as a volume‑expanding agent used to restore or maintain intravascular fluid volume. This classification reflects its role in clinical situations such as hemorrhage, hypovolemia, or other conditions requiring rapid hemodynamic support, where augmenting plasma volume is essential. Recognizing tromethamin as part of the Blood Products/Modifiers/Volume Expanders class helps clinicians select appropriate volume‑expanding therapies and understand its mechanism of modifying blood components to increase circulatory volume.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2586
retrieved_docs.content: Blood transfusion, referred to in Arabic as “نقل الدم”, is classified as a specific physical treatment method. It falls under the category of Physical Treatment Methods, which in turn is a subcategory of “المعالجة الفيزيائية” (physical therapy). As a therapeutic intervention involving the transfer of blood components, نقل الدم is an essential procedure in many clinical settings. However, it is also recognized as a cause of complications of surgical procedures or other medical care, underscoring the importance of careful indication, monitoring, and management to minimize adverse outcomes. Understanding its hierarchical placement within physical treatment modalities and its potential risk profile is crucial for optimizing patient safety and treatment effectiveness.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 4 -- Community 2694
retrieved_docs.content: Dextran 70, a high‑molecular‑weight plasma volume expander, is contraindicated in patients with الاعتلال الخثري (thrombotic disease). Because Dextran 70 can increase blood viscosity and promote coagulation, its use in individuals who already have a propensity for clot formation may exacerbate the underlying thrombotic condition and increase the risk of serious vascular complications. Clinicians should therefore avoid administering Dextran 70 to patients diagnosed with الاعتلال الخثري and consider alternative volume‑expanding therapies that do not carry this pro‑thrombotic risk.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 109
retrieved_docs.content: disorders, and obstetric interventions together form a comprehensive framework for risk assessment, care planning, and management of maternal‑fetal outcomes during pregnancy, delivery, and the postpartum period.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2249
retrieved_docs.content: INJECTIONS AND IMPLANTATIONS is categorized under تسريب, which itself is placed within the broader group of الإجراءات العلاجية، غير مصنف في موضع آخر (therapeutic procedures not classified elsewhere). تسريب is defined as a type of نقل الدم (blood transfusion), linking INJECTIONS AND IMPLANTATIONS to the blood transfusion domain. In addition to its relationship with تسريب, INJECTIONS AND IMPLANTATIONS is also associated with فصادة الكريات البيض (leukapheresis) and with نقل الدم الذاتي (autologous blood transfusion), reflecting its relevance to multiple specialized transfusion and apheresis procedures. نقل الدم serves as a parent category for نقل الدم الذاتي, indicating that self‑blood transfusion is a specific sub‑type of general blood transfusion. Physical Treatment Methods is likewise positioned under نقل الدم, suggesting that certain physical therapeutic techniques are considered part of the transfusion‑related treatment spectrum. The concept of blood transfusion is used to classify نقل الدم, reinforcing the hierarchical relationship between the general practice of transfusion and its specific categories. Finally, blood transfusion is recognized as a potential source of complications of surgical procedures or medical care, highlighting the need for careful management of transfusion‑related risks in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2820
retrieved_docs.content: INJECTIONS AND IMPLANTATIONS are classified as subprocedures within the broader categories of فصادة الكريات البيض (white‑blood‑cell apheresis) and نقل الدم الذاتي (autologous blood transfusion). Both parent procedures involve the manipulation, collection, or reinfusion of blood components, and the inclusion of INJECTIONS AND IMPLANTATIONS under these headings underscores their role in delivering therapeutic agents or devices directly into the patient’s circulatory system or tissues as part of blood‑related therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 4 -- Community 2838
retrieved_docs.content: Blood transfusion, referred to in Arabic as نقل الدم, is classified as a type of Physical Treatment Methods, placing it within the broader category of physical therapeutic interventions. While blood transfusion can be a valuable clinical procedure, it is also recognized as a potential cause of complications associated with surgical procedures or other medical care. Awareness of its classification under physical treatment methods and its possible role in postoperative or procedural complications is essential for clinicians to manage risks and optimize patient outcomes.
